--- SOURCE 4: Lignocellulosic biomass ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lignocellulosic_biomass

SUMMARY:
It is composed of two kinds of carbohydrate polymers, cellulose and hemicellulose, and an aromatic-rich polymer called lignin.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Lignocellulosic biomass

**Lignocellulose**  refers to plant dry matter ([biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass")), so called lignocellulosic biomass. It is the most abundantly available raw material on the Earth for the production of [biofuels](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) It is composed of two kinds of carbohydrate polymers, [cellulose](/wiki/Cellulose "Cellulose") and [hemicellulose](/wiki/Hemicellulose "Hemicellulose"), and an aromatic-rich polymer called [lignin](/wiki/Lignin "Lignin").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Any biomass rich in cellulose, hemicelluloses, and lignin are commonly referred to as lignocellulosic biomass.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Each component has a distinct chemical behavior. Being a composite of three very different components makes the processing of lignocellulose challenging. The evolved resistance to degradation or even separation is referred to as recalcitrance. Overcoming this recalcitrance to produce useful, high value products requires a combination of heat, chemicals, enzymes, and microorganisms.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) These carbohydrate-containing polymers contain different sugar monomers (six and five carbon sugars) and they are covalently bound to lignin.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Proserpine-Sugar-Mill-rail-tracks-1188.jpg/500px-Proserpine-Sugar-Mill-rail-tracks-1188.jpg)

Lignocellulosic biomass can be broadly classified as virgin biomass, waste biomass, and [energy crops](/wiki/Energy_crops "Energy crops"). Virgin biomass includes plants. Waste biomass is produced as a low value byproduct of various industrial sectors such as [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") ([corn stover](/wiki/Corn_stover "Corn stover"), sugarcane [bagasse](/wiki/Bagasse "Bagasse"), straw etc.) and [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry") ([saw mill](/wiki/Saw_mill "Saw mill") and [paper mill](/wiki/Paper_mill "Paper mill") discards). [Energy crops](/wiki/Energy_crops "Energy crops") are crops with a high yield of lignocellulosic biomass produced as a raw material for the production of second-generation biofuel; examples include switchgrass (*[Panicum virgatum](/wiki/Panicum_virgatum "Panicum virgatum")*) and [elephant grass](/wiki/Miscanthus_%C3%97_giganteus "Miscanthus × giganteus"). The biofuels generated from these energy crops are sources of sustainable energy.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Contents

## Chemical composition

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Xylan_hardwood.svg/500px-Xylan_hardwood.svg.png)

Lignocellulose consists of three components, each with properties that pose challenges to commercial applications.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Dedicated energy crops

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Miscanthus_Bestand.JPG/500px-Miscanthus_Bestand.JPG)

Many crops are of interest for their ability to provide high yields of biomass. Some can be harvested multiple times each year. These include [poplar](/wiki/Populus "Populus") trees and *[Miscanthus giganteus](/wiki/Miscanthus_giganteus "Miscanthus giganteus")*. The premier energy crop is [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane"), which is a source of the readily fermentable [sucrose](/wiki/Sucrose "Sucrose") and the lignocellulosic by-product [bagasse](/wiki/Bagasse "Bagasse").

## Application

### Pulp and paper industry

Lignocellulosic biomass is the feedstock for the [pulp and paper industry](/wiki/Pulp_and_paper_industry "Pulp and paper industry"). In this process lignin and hemicellulose are typically separated from the plant material leaving the fibrous cellulose component to be processed for paper production, or 'chemical cellulose'.[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) Through the pulp process most of the lignin is removed and discharged as waste material in the form of effluent/wastewater before then being used as low-value fuel to generate electricity and heat.[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) In principle, the world’s current sugar demand could be fulfilled by repurposing pulp and paper mills for lignocellulosic sugar production, making it a promising [resilient food](/w/index.php?title=Resilient_food&action=edit&redlink=1 "Resilient food (page does not exist)").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Biofuels

Lignocellulosic biomass, in the form of [wood fuel](/wiki/Wood_fuel "Wood fuel"), has a long history as a source of energy. Since the middle of the 20th century, the interest of biomass as a precursor to *liquid* fuels has increased. To be specific, the [fermentation](/wiki/Ethanol_fermentation "Ethanol fermentation") of lignocellulosic biomass to [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol")[[15]](#cite_note-15) is an attractive route to fuels that supplements the [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuels "Fossil fuels"). Biomass can be a [carbon neutral](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") source of energy in the long run. However depending on the source of biomass, it will not be carbon neutral in the short term. For instance if the biomass is derived from trees, the time period to regrow the tree (on the order of decades) will see a net increase in carbon dioxide in the [Earth's atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Earth "Atmosphere of Earth") upon the combustion of lignocellulosic ethanol. However, if woody material from annual crop residue is used, the fuel could be considered carbon-neutral. Aside from ethanol, many other lignocellulose-derived fuels are of potential interest, including [butanol](/wiki/Butanol "Butanol"), [dimethylfuran](/wiki/2,5-Dimethylfuran "2,5-Dimethylfuran"), and [gamma-valerolactone](/wiki/Gamma-Valerolactone "Gamma-Valerolactone").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

One barrier to the production of ethanol from biomass is that the sugars necessary for fermentation are trapped inside the lignocellulose. Lignocellulose has evolved to resist degradation and to confer [hydrolytic](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") stability and structural robustness to the cell walls of the plants. This robustness or "recalcitrance" is attributable to the crosslinking between the polysaccharides (cellulose and hemicellulose) and the lignin via [ester](/wiki/Ester "Ester") and [ether](/wiki/Ether "Ether") linkages.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Ester linkages arise between oxidized sugars, the [uronic acids](/wiki/Uronic_acid "Uronic acid"), and the phenols and phenylpropanols functionalities of the lignin. To extract the fermentable sugars, one must first disconnect the celluloses from the lignin, and then use acid or enzymatic methods to [hydrolyze](/wiki/Hydrolyze "Hydrolyze") the newly freed celluloses to break them down into simple monosaccharides. Another challenge to [biomass fermentation](/wiki/Biomass_fermentation "Biomass fermentation") is the high percentage of pentoses in the hemicellulose, such as [xylose](/wiki/Xylose "Xylose"), or wood sugar. Unlike hexoses such as glucose, pentoses are difficult to ferment. The problems presented by the lignin and hemicellulose fractions are the foci of much contemporary research.

A large sector of research into the exploitation of lignocellulosic biomass as a feedstock for bio-ethanol focuses particularly on the fungus *[Trichoderma reesei](/wiki/Trichoderma_reesei "Trichoderma reesei")*, known for its cellulolytic abilities. Multiple avenues are being explored including the design of an optimised cocktail of cellulases and hemicellulases isolated from *T. reesei*, as well as genetic-engineering-based strain improvement to allow the fungus to simply be placed in the presence of lignocellulosic biomass and break down the matter into [D-glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose") [monomers](/wiki/Monomer "Monomer").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Strain improvement methods have led to strains capable of producing significantly more cellulases than the original QM6a isolate; certain industrial strains are known to produce up to 100g of cellulase per litre of fungus,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] thus allowing for maximal extraction of sugars from lignocellulosic biomass. These sugars can then be fermented, leading to bio-ethanol.

## Research

Some chemicals could be obtained from lignocellulosic biomass. Almost all are derived from the sugars obtained by hydrolysis of the cellulose component.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Lignocellulosic biomasses has been considered in the production of biocomposites materials such as particle panels, wood-plastic composites, and cement/[geopolymer](/wiki/Geopolymer "Geopolymer") wood composites. Even though the production of biocomposite materials rely mostly on wood resources, in less forest-covered countries or in countries where wood resources are already being overused, it is possible to utilize alternative sources of biomass such as invasive plants, agricultural and sawmills residues for the creation of new "green" composites.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Biocomposites produced with lignocellulosic biomass as an alternative to conventional materials, are attracting attention because they are renewable and cheaper but also because they fit perfectly into the policy of the "cascade utilization" of the resources.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Cellulose, a polysaccharide, constitutes 20-50% of lignocellulosic biomass. It provides structural integrity to plant cell walls, making it a vital component for biofuel production.
Hemicellulose accounts for 15-35% of biomass and is a heterogeneous polymer. It aids in binding cellulose fibers and contributes to the overall stability of the biomass structure.
Lignin, making up 5-30% of biomass, is an aromatic polymer that provides rigidity and resistance to degradation, complicating the processing of lignocellulosic materials.
Lignocellulosic biomass sources include agricultural residues (like corn stover) and forest residues (such as sawdust), representing significant low-cost feedstock for bioenergy.

enriched_points.point_title: Challenges in processing due to lignin
enriched_points.expanded_content: Lignin's complex structure and crosslinking with cellulose create recalcitrance, posing challenges in biomass processing, necessitating advanced methods for effective degradation and sugar extraction.
